data.title: Celestica: The Quiet AI Hardware Powerhouse
data.description:
Celestica Inc. (NYSE:CLS) may not be a household name, but this Toronto-based electronics manufacturer has quietly positioned itself as a big winner in some of today’s hottest technology and industrial trends. In many ways, Celestica is an “under-the-radar picks and shovels” play, a company supplying the critical hardware behind booming markets like AI data centers, advanced computing, aerospace and defense, and more. Although Celestica’s shares have left many celebrated tech names in the dust lately, they still sit roughly a fifth below their former peak. The market hasn’t yet caught on to how thoroughly the company has reinvented itself, or to the steady growth engine now humming beneath the surface.

Strong Financial Momentum

In the first quarter of 2024, Celestica grew revenue by 20% YoY to $2.21 billion in Q1. Celestica also generated a strong free cash flow of $65 million, which was an impressive increase of $9 million a year prior. The company raised its full-year outlook in April 2024 and forecasts a 14% higher revenue of $9.1 billion. Over the 2022 to 2024 span Celestica averaged roughly 20% annual revenue growth (from $5.6 billion in 2021 to $9.6 billion in 2024) while expanding GAAP earnings by an astonishing ~65% per year. Few companies of this size are posting that kind of performance, and it speaks to a business with serious momentum.

Exposure to Secular Growth Trends

Celestica’s momentum is fueled by broad, long-running tailwinds that continue to gather pace. The major cloud platforms are locked in an AI build-out frenzy, rushing to cram ever-more compute power into their data centers. That race is channeling extraordinary sums into physical infrastructure: Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL) and Microsoft (MSFT) together are expected to sink about $320 billion into AI-driven data-center projects in 2025. This tidal wave of investment directly benefits Celestica as many of these hyperscale cloud companies are major customers.

Celestica’s Connectivity & Cloud Solutions segment grew a remarkable 38% in Q1 2024, with segment profit margins widening to around 7%. Management noted robust demand from hyperscale and “next-gen” computing programs as a key driver. In practice, Celestica isn’t just assembling generic circuit boards; it has become a strategic partner helping the tech titans roll out cutting-edge infrastructure. The company recently won contracts to design and produce entire AI-optimized hardware racks for multiple large cloud clients. Celestica should see a steady flow of business as long as the AI arms race continues to ramp up. Given the pace at which frontier AI models continue to develop, Celestica likely has nothing to worry about on this front.

Advanced computing demand isn’t confined to the Googles and Amazons of the world. Celestica also serves sectors like high-performance computing, enterprise storage and semiconductor capital equipment, which are benefiting from the same digital tailwinds. The company’s expertise spans data-center design, networking gear, semiconductor test equipment and more, giving it multiple touchpoints to growth in the broader electronics ecosystem. Notably, Celestica’s capital-equipment business (part of its ATS segment) includes manufacturing for semiconductor and electronics-equipment makers, so the ongoing investment in chip fabrication and advanced electronics production is another indirect boon. In short, whether it’s assembling the latest AI server racks, telecom 5G gear or sophisticated chip-making machinery, Celestica has positioned itself squarely in the path of secular tech growth.

Beyond the digital realm, Celestica is also leveraged to a renaissance in aerospace and defense (A&D) manufacturing. After years of underproduction, the commercial-aerospace industry faces a massive order backlog as airlines scramble to upgrade fleets. Airbus recently reported a backlog of over 8,650 jets, near an all-time high, and Boeing’s order book is similarly huge at about 6,200 aircraft.

Likewise, global defense spending is on the rise, hitting a record $2.72 trillion in 2024. Defense contractors are investing in production of everything from avionic systems to radar and communications, all areas where Celestica’s high-reliability manufacturing capabilities come into play. While Celestica’s A&D unit saw modest declines last year due to some program timing and supply-chain constraints, the longer-term trajectory looks positive given these secular tailwinds. Management expects the A&D business to return to growth as backlogs translate into production ramps, and in Q1 2025 this segment already showed a 5% year-over-year uptick and improved margins.

Underappreciated Catalyst: Outsourcing and Complexity

A less obvious yet powerful engine in Celestica’s ascent is the accelerating shift by original-equipment makers toward outsourcing their most intricate hardware work. As global supply chains grow more tangled and leading-edge designs demand deep, specialized engineering talent, many brands no longer want to shoulder every manufacturing step in-house. Instead, they turn to partners like Celestica. Analysts expect the electronics-manufacturing-services market to nearly double (from about $600 billion in 2024 to more than $1 trillion early next decade) because of this hand-off. Celestica is capturing an outsized share of that expansion by zeroing in on the hard stuff: precision avionics builds for aircraft, entire AI-server chassis for hyperscale clouds, and other projects where complexity commands premium economics. The proof is in the numbers: while the typical EMS firm creeps along at mid-single-digit growth, Celestica’s revenue has been climbing roughly 20% a year, showing it is winning both new accounts and a larger slice of existing customers’ budgets.

Its ability to offer end-to-end solutions, from design and engineering through to assembly and supply-chain logistics, makes Celestica more of a strategic partner than a simple contract manufacturer. As a result, the company can command better margins and stickier customer relationships than the old stereotype of a low-margin “build-to-print” supplier. Indeed, Celestica’s evolving business mix has steadily lifted its profitability: the company’s adjusted operating margin hit the mid-6% range in early 2024 (a record level for them) up from roughly 5% a couple of years ago. Return on invested capital has likewise climbed (to about 25% in Q1 2024), reflecting both operational improvements and savvy capital management. All of this supports a higher valuation than a typical EMS firm, a fact the market has only slowly started to acknowledge.

Valuation and Upside Potential

Despite Celestica’s heady growth and improving quality of earnings, the stock still looks reasonably priced. Even after a strong run, Celestica trades at only about 1.3x trailing revenue and roughly 15 to 16x EBITDA on an enterprise-value basis. For context, the broader tech-hardware sector often trades at higher multiples, and those valuations don’t usually come with 20% growth attached. Celestica’s price-to-earnings ratio, too, remains modest given its trajectory, roughly in the mid-teens on forward earnings by some estimates.

This suggests the market is skeptical that Celestica’s recent performance is sustainable, or perhaps it still categorizes the company as a low-growth, low-margin manufacturer. This creates an opening for a differentiated thesis: namely, that Celestica deserves a re-rating upward as it proves its durability. We’ve already seen evidence of that durability. After Q1 2024’s big beat, management raised guidance for the year; by the time Q4 rolled around, Celestica had achieved 21% full-year revenue growth and a 58% jump in non-GAAP EPS for 2024, far above what most analysts had expected at the start of the year. And yet the stock, as noted, hasn’t run away in price, it’s still trading below many target prices and far below where its growth rate might justify.

Looking ahead, Celestica now forecasts another year of double-digit growth in 2025, with revenue expected to reach roughly $10.7 billion (implying about 13% growth) and earnings per share climbing over 20%. If those targets are met, Celestica’s EPS would be around $5, putting the current share price (as of mid-2025) at a very undemanding multiple. In a market that has been willing to award 20x, 25x, even 30x P/E multiples to companies with far less growth, a case can be made that Celestica’s stock has room to run. Continued execution, delivering on the forecasted 2025 results or winning additional marquee contracts in AI and aerospace, could be catalysts for investors to narrow this valuation gap. Additionally, Celestica’s substantial share-buyback program indicates confidence in its undervaluation and will further boost per-share metrics going forward.

Risks and Challenges

Perhaps the biggest near-term risk is customer concentration. Celestica’s recent success has been tied to a few very large customers in the cloud/AI domain; in 2024, about 39% of its revenue came from just three hyperscaler clients. This dependence is a double-edged sword: it has enabled rapid growth, but if even one of those giants delays orders or switches to a competitor, Celestica’s results could take a hit. Some analysts have flagged this reliance on hyperscalers (along with exposure to global-trade conditions) as a potential vulnerability for revenue and margins. It’s worth noting that tech-capex cycles can be lumpy; the current AI-server boom could moderate if, for example, cloud companies decide to digest their recent investments or face economic pressure to curb spending. Celestica must work to broaden its customer base and end-market mix to mitigate this risk, and it is doing so, as evidenced by landing a second major hyperscaler and a new “digital-native” customer for its AI-hardware solutions recently. Still, investors should monitor the concentration closely.

Another challenge is that not all of Celestica’s segments are booming at once. The Advanced Technology Solutions division, which includes industrial and automotive-related business, has seen softness; for instance, demand for electric-vehicle charging equipment slowed in 2024 due to an inventory backlog in that sector. Cyclical dips in areas like industrial or capital equipment could partially offset growth in cloud and aerospace in any given quarter. Celestica’s worldwide footprint also means it lives with the same big-picture headaches that dog every border-hopping manufacturer: parts prices that can jump overnight, shipping logjams, and the geopolitical sparring that can reshape trade flows in a flash. The company has handled the recent supply-chain storms with notable poise, leaning on factories in North America, Europe, and Asia to reroute work when one region stalls. Still, shareholders should remember that profit margins can sway if component costs spike or if new tariffs or export rules force an abrupt reshuffle of where the firm builds its gear.

The Differentiated Bull Case

What makes the Celestica opportunity compelling, and arguably under-appreciated, is the confluence of exceptional execution and multi-year megatrends, wrapped in a valuation that has yet to catch up. In other words, the market’s cautious view, shaped by Celestica’s legacy as a low-margin contract manufacturer or by fears of a one-off AI-spending blip, may be behind the curve. Celestica today is a very different company than it was a decade ago: it has moved up-market to become an integral partner for top-tier tech and aerospace players, it’s delivering sustained high growth with improving margins, and it’s demonstrated the ability to generate cash and return it to shareholders. Management’s confidence is evident, not only in their raised forecasts but in actions like share buybacks and strategic acquisitions.

If Celestica can continue to broaden its customer mix, reducing the hyperscaler-concentration risk, and capitalize on secular tailwinds, there’s a strong case that earnings will keep rising at a healthy clip in the coming years. Even assuming some normalization after the current super-cycle, the company’s exposure across AI, cloud, defense and other growth markets provides multiple avenues for upside surprises. This is a company that just grew EPS nearly 60% in one year and yet trades at a valuation of $13 billion that suggests little growth at all, a discrepancy that won’t last if Celestica delivers on its promises.

data.title: Magnolia Oil & Gas: Strong Performance From Its Giddings Wells
data.description:
Magnolia Oil & Gas (NYSE:MGY) delivered strong Q1 2025 production results with total production around 3% above its guidance. As a result of its well outperformance and improved capital efficiency, Magnolia has also raised its full-year total production guidance by 2% while reducing its capex budget by $25 million.

Magnolia's outperformance has been driven by natural gas, and its oil production guidance has remained unchanged. Nonetheless, the natural gas outperformance is still useful given improved natural gas pricing.

I now expect Magnolia to generate $410 million in 2025 free cash flow at current strip, much of which is likely to go towards share repurchases.

I've lowered my long-term oil price by $5 to $70, so that has a negative impact on Magnolia's estimated value. This is partially offset by Magnolia's capital efficiency improvements and gas outperformance though.

I now estimate Magnolia's value at $25 per share, compared to a prior estimate of $27.

Q1 2025 Results

Magnolia's Q1 2025 production outperformed expectations. It delivered 96,549 BOEPD in average production, which was 3% above its guidance for 94,000 BOEPD in Q1 2025 production. Magnolia's average total production also grew by 4% compared to Q4 2024.

This strong performance was driven by its Giddings assets, which saw 7% production growth compared to Q4 2024 amidst shallower than expected well declines combined with excellent initial well productivity.

The outperformance was mostly with natural gas volumes, as oil production volumes grew by a smaller 1% compared to Q4 2024.

Magnolia's strong production was achieved with $130.4 million in Q1 2025 D&C capex, slightly under its $135 million budget for the quarter. Magnolia noted that Q1 was expected to be its highest capex quarter in 2025.

Magnolia generated $110.5 million in free cash flow during the quarter, of which it returned $81.7 million to shareholders in the form of its regular $0.15 per share quarterly dividend and also share repurchases. Magnolia repurchased 2.2 million shares in Q1 2025 for a total of $52 million.

Magnolia's balance sheet remains quite healthy with $247.6 million in cash at the end of Q1 2025 versus $400 million in outstanding 6.875% unsecured notes due 2032.

Increased Expectations For Gas Production

Magnolia noted that its stronger than expected Q1 2025 production and its overall well outperformance gives it confidence that it can deliver higher total production levels with less capex. It has thus increased its 2025 total production guidance by 2% to a new range of 7% to 9% year-over-year growth, while lowering its capex budget by approximately $25 million.

Magnolia is maintaining its 2025 oil production growth expectations in the low-single digit range, and the improved production is being driven by natural gas.

Magnolia mentioned that it brought online two multi-well pads in Q1 2025 that were in a more gas-heavy area of its Giddings asset. The timing was to take advantage of seasonally stronger natural gas prices and the wells exceeded early production expectations along with having a shallower decline profile. The solid oil production plus high amounts of natural gas production from these wells have resulted in fast projected payback periods.

Magnolia ended up realizing $3.11 per Mcf for its natural gas in Q1 2025, more than double what it realized in Q1 2024. Natural gas prices are expected to remain significantly better than 2024 levels.

Updated 2025 Outlook

Magnolia now expects approximately 97,000 BOEPD in 2025 production, up from its earlier guidance for approximately 95,000 BOEPD in average production. Magnolia's oil production is expected to be around 39,500 barrels per day.

The current strip for 2025 includes $63 to $64 WTI oil and $3.80 Henry Hub natural gas. Magnolia is projected to generate $1.298 billion in 2025 revenues at those prices.

Type Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 14,417,500 $62.50 $901
NGLs 9,864,125 $20.00 $197
Gas 66,740,250 $3.00 $200
Total Revenues $1,298

Magnolia's 2025 capex budget is now approximately $450 million with the $25 million reduction. Its improved capital efficiency has allowed it to defer the completion of several wells into 2026 while raising its production guidance.

Magnolia is now projected to generate $410 million in free cash flow during 2025.

$ Million
Lease Operating $187
Gathering, Transportation, and Processing $60
Taxes Other Than Income $71
Cash G&A $70
Net Cash Interest $15
Capex $450
Cash Income Taxes $35
Total $888

Magnolia's $0.15 per share quarterly dividend may add up to around $115 million in 2025, so it would have close to $300 million of its free cash flow remaining for share repurchases (and acquisitions) before using any of its cash on hand.

Notes On Valuation

I had previously estimated Magnolia's value at $27 per share at long-term (after 2025) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas.

I now estimate Magnolia's value at approximately $25 per share at long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas.

The reduction in Magnolia's estimated value is primarily due to the $5 decrease in my long-term oil price. This is partially offset by Magnolia's outperformance in natural gas production and its capital efficiency improvements.

The combined impact of those various changes (long-term oil prices, natural gas production and capex) is to reduce Magnolia's projected free cash flow by around $40 million per year.

Conclusion

Magnolia's Q1 2025 report involved strong production results that allowed it to boost its full-year total production guidance by 2%. This was driven by gas outperformance, while oil production expectations remained unchanged.

Magnolia's improved capital efficiency also resulted in it being able to reduce its capex budget by $25 million. Magnolia is now expected to generate $410 million in 2025 free cash flow despite being unhedged and dealing with an average of low-to-mid $60s WTI oil in 2025.

I have trimmed my long-term oil price expectation to $70 from $75 though, so that reduces Magnolia's value a bit despite its capital efficiency improvements and strong well performance. I now estimate Magnolia's value at $25 per share at long-term $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

data.title: Zealand: Promising Roche-Partnered Obesity Drug Developer - But Stock Is Pricey
data.description:
Investment Overview

Zealand Pharma A/S (OTCPK:ZLDPF), founded in 1997 and headquartered in Soborg, Denmark, is one of the more advanced players in the emerging obesity treatment market, estimated by some to be worth >$200bn by 2031, with 16 new drugs on the market.

The current leaders - who market and sell the two commercially approved obesity drug molecules, semaglutide and tirzepatide, or Wegovy and Zepbound (also both approved to treat Type 2 Diabetes as Ozempic / Mounjaro) - are Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY).

Semaglutide earned revenues of ~$25bn - $16.8bn from Ozempic and $8.1bn from Wegovy - in 2024, while tirzepatide generated revenues of $16.5bn - $11.5bn from Mounjaro, and $5bn from Zepbound. In Q125, Ozempic earned ~$5bn revenues, up 18% year-on-year, Wegovy $2.65bn, up 85%, Mounjaro $3.84bn, up 113%, and Zepbound $2.3bn, up ~350%.

It's no surprise then that rival Big Pharma companies are desperate to develop similar drugs to challenge Novo and Lilly, and potentially add tens of billions of dollars to their top lines.

This is what prompted Swiss Pharma giant Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) to pay $1.4bn upfront, further payments of $250m per annum for the next two years, and potentially, additional milestone payments that could bring the total consideration to $5.3bn, to enter into a collaboration and license agreement with Zealand, and to co-develop and co-commercialize its lead obesity drug candidate petrelintide as "a future foundational therapy for people with overweight and obesity."

Petrelintide is, according to a press release announcing the deal on march 12th, this year:

A long-acting amylin analog suitable for once-weekly subcutaneous administration that has been designed with chemical and physical stability with no fibrillation around neutral pH, allowing for co-formulation and co-administration with other peptides.

The release goes on:

Amylin is produced in the pancreatic beta cells and co-secreted with insulin in response to ingested nutrients. Amylin receptor activation has been shown to reduce body weight by restoring sensitivity to the satiety hormone leptin, inducing a sense of feeling full faster.

Current clinical data or pre-clinical data suggest a potential of petrelintide to deliver weight loss comparable to GLP-1 receptor agonists but with improved tolerability for a better patient experience and high-quality weight loss.

Zealand announced its Q1 2025 earnings last week - revenues came to ~$1.2m, which was "driven by the license and development agreement for Zegalogue with Novo Nordisk", Zealand told analysts on its earnings call, with the Roche money due to be accounted for next quarter. Operating expenses came to ~$59m, meaning net loss recorded was ~$(57.5m).

Zealand's current market cap is $4.3bn, its shares having risen in value from ~$12 in mid-2022, to a high of >$130 in July 2024, and currently trading at ~$60, after a difficult bear run that has seen the stock price slip by nearly 45% across the past 6 months.

Now. let's take a deeper dive look at the company, and try to evaluate whether investing in the company would be wise at this time.

Zealand - Company & Pipeline Overview Aside From Petrelintide

In its Q1 2025 earnings presentation, Zealand shares research that 50% of adults globally are expected to be overweight or obese by 2030, underlining the massive market opportunity that awaits any company that can develop a safe and effective anti-obesity drug.

As we can see above, Zealand is about much more than simply petrelintide - there are numerous GLP-1 / GIP receptor agonist drugs drugs in development, a rare disease drug at the registrational stage, and different indications targeted.

Below is a definition of GLP / GIP taken from an abstract paper shared via the National Library of Medicine's website:

Gastric inhibitory polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon‐like peptide‐1 (GLP‐1) are the two primary incretin hormones secreted from the intestine on ingestion of glucose or nutrients to stimulate insulin secretion from pancreatic β cells.

GIP and GLP‐1 exert their effects by binding to their specific receptors, the GIP receptor (GIPR) and the GLP‐1 receptor (GLP‐1R), which belong to the G‐protein coupled receptor family.

Receptor binding activates and increases the level of intracellular cyclic adenosine monophosphate in pancreatic β cells, thereby stimulating insulin secretion glucose‐dependently.

A by-product of these effects is reduced appetite - essentially, GLP-1 receptor agonists and GLP / GIP "mimetics" (so called because they mimic the satiating effects of the hormone incretin) help people feel more full and eat less, which helps them lose weight.

As we can see, Zealand has survodutide, a once-weekly, injectable, glucagon / GLP-1 receptor agonist in a Phase 3 study in obesity, and in Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatohepatitis ("MASH"), alongside its partner Boehringer Ingelheim. It was this drug's potential that initially drove Zealand's phenomenal share price gains.

CEO Adam Steensberg told analysts on last week's earnings call:

We look forward to top-line data from the first Phase 3 trial in people with overweight or obesity anticipated in the first half of 2026.

Boehringer Ingelheim expects to launch survodutide in 2027 or 2028 and if successful survodutide could be the third incretin-based therapy to market and it would be the first approved dual glucagon GLP-1 receptor agonist in this exciting era of novel weight loss therapies.

You might expect such a significant catalyst to even put the Roche deal in the shade, however semaglutide and tirzepatide, especially, have set a high bar. Tirzepatide achieved >20% mean weight loss in patients in its Phase 3 study, while Boehringer has apparently reported Phase 2 data showing:

almost 40% of patients who were overweight or obese (NCT04667377) and who received survodutide achieved more than a 20% reduction in body weight after 46 weeks of treatment.

While these data are impressive, that seemingly falls short of the bar set by tirzepatide, so, even if approved, perhaps "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) revenues may prove elusive. Unfortunately, the same may apply in MASH, despite Zealand touting "best-in-class" Phase 2 data, as tirzepatide / semaglutide will likely gain approval in MASH, also.

Dapiglutide has "potential for complementary anti-inflammatory effects from GLP-1 agonism and GLP-2 agonism," Zealand says, and "detailed data" from both Part 1 and Part 2 of a Phase 1 study will be shared next month, management says.

Dasiglucagon has been the recipient of two "Complete Response Letters" ("CRL") from the FDA, essentially denying its approval application, the latest being related to manufacturing site deficiencies. Management is hopeful an approval will be secured, in time, however, this being an ultra-rare disease, the revenue opportunity will be limited. The same is likely true of glepaglutide.

Petrelintide - What Persuaded Roche To Invest Billions In This Candidate?

On the Q1 2025 earnings call, Zealand's CEO commented:

We believe that amylin analogs host the potential to become the future leading category for weight management. The increasing body of clinical evidence supporting the safety tolerability and efficacy of amylin analogs have derisked the amylin, the petrelintide program as well.

Thus in petrelintide, we are, based on the clinical data reported to date and due to the molecular specific attributes, confident in a best-in-class potential and thus a unique opportunity to build the leading amylin-based franchise for weight management.

Roche clearly agrees with this sentiment, but does the data support it? First let's consider an independent definition of Amylin, as per ScienceDirect.com:

Amylin is a peptide hormone released from the islets of Langerhans in response to a meal.31 Peripherally, it is a potent anorexia agent, producing its effects through the nucleus tractus solitarus. Amylin knockout mice have hyperphagia and increased weight, and amylin decreased food intake in humans with diabetes mellitus.

Zealand's Chief Medical Officer ("CMO") commented on the earnings call:

We remain committed to advancing petrelintide as standalone therapy targeting 15% to 20% weight loss and better patient experience with improved gastrointestinal and overall tolerability and acceptability.

We also maintain that petrelintide has the potential as a best-in-class therapy as this asset is both physically and chemically stable with no fibrillation at physiologic pH and petrelintide was developed so that it can be co-administered and co-formulated with other peptide-based therapies.

As a monotherapy, then, Zealand believes petrelintide could break the 20% weight-loss barrier, which would put it on a par with tirzepatide / Zepbound, however, we may note that Novo Nordisk promised something similar from its own amylin-targeting candidate, Cagrisema, which combines cagrilinitide, a dual amylin agonist, and semaglutide.

In fact, Novo promised the drug would achieve >25% mean weight loss, but ultimately, the drug fell well short of that mark, showing a figure of 15.7% after 68 weeks.

Zealand and Roche will also try combining petrelintide with CT-388, Roche's once-weekly, subcutaneous dual GLP-1 and GIP receptor modulator, however this drug is still in the clinical trial process and its unclear if it will match tirzepatide's safety or efficacy profile.

Zealand is running monotherapy studies ZUPREME-1 - in obese patients, and now fully enrolled, and ZUPREME-2, in patients with obesity and coexisting Type 2 diabetes. Data from both studies will be available next year.

The reality is that developing GLP-1, GIP, or amylin - which appears to have very similar qualities to the first two peptides - drug candidates is not straightforward. We should remember that what Lilly and Novo have achieved with tirzepatide and semaglutide was once considered impossible by the scientific community.

Hence, while Roche has invested billions into developing similar drugs, success is not guaranteed, even if, as a Roche investor myself, I hope the Pharma does succeed.

What - in my opinion - Roche has purchased from Zealand is a candidate that is a prospect, not a "slam dunk," for approval and eventual "best-in-class" status.

If that status is achieved, the >$5bn Roche pays to Zealand will soon be forgotten, and the deal will be remembered as one of the great Pharma M&A deals, but to say there is compelling data suggesting this will happen is, in my opinion at least, not quite true at this stage.

Concluding Thoughts - Where Is Zealand's Share Price Headed?

If we think about companies developing late stage GLP drugs, Viking Therapeutics (VKTX) has a market cap of $3.2bn, Metsera (MTSR), >$3bn and Structure Therapeutics (GCPR), >$1bn, while others such as Terns Pharmaceuticals (TERN) and Septerna (SEPN) are valued <$500m.

To me this suggests that Zealand's share price has huge potential volatility - if survodutide or petrelintide deliver some strong data in 2026, when most of the key readouts are due, I suspect it could increase Zealand's market cap by anything from 50% - 100%, as the company would be taking a significant step towards commercial revenues / milestone payouts / tiered royalties, with the largest of all pharmaceutical markets in play.

If data disappoints, however, I can see the Zealand Pharma A/S share price tumbling and Roche ending up with egg on its face - its deal with Zealand only relates to a single asset. Failure is not uncommon in the GLP space - Pfizer (PFE) has abandoned development of its oral candidate, danuglipron, while Novo's valuation has been almost halved by the Cagrisema disappointment. Furthermore, Altimmune's share price has fallen from ~$25, to ~$5, since mid-2022, and Viking stock is down >60% on a 1-year basis.

As such, I don't see myself rushing out to buy Zealand stock at this time. We should also note that Pharma giants Merck (MRK) and AstraZeneca have entered the development race, partnering with Chinese biotechs, Amgen (AMGN) has a challenger with a unique MoA, Novo has announced a partnership with Septerna, and Lilly's pipeline of "next-generation drugs, including the "triple-G" agonist retatrutide, looks formidable.

Bearing all of this in mind, and holding Roche and Novo stock as I am, that is enough exposure to the GLP phenomena for me for the time being. I can understand why Zealand is the highest value non-Big Pharma" company in the GLP / obesity space, but I am on the sidelines until I see more data.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NetScout: Steady Progress As Expected
data.description:
The last time I wrote about NetScout Systems (NASDAQ: NTCT), my position was that this is a company with great growth prospects. Why? Well, it has solid foundations in terms of high-margin recurring revenue, operations, leadership, and cost management. Three months and another earnings report down the line, my confidence is only growing, even though the stock price has dipped a bit since then.

Full-year results for FY2025 are in, and they’re largely on the positive side. Earnings came in higher than the market expected even though its Service Assurance business had some challenges. Cybersecurity was the main driver of that growth, though, at +7% YoY for the year. But the company missed its non-GAAP EPS by $1.01. Still, I believe that the signs are there that NetScout is making good on its potential entering fiscal year 2026. Let’s talk about why.

Demand in Cybersecurity and Enterprise is Still Solid

Clearly, NetScout’s main growth vehicle is its cybersecurity enterprise solutions. After all, total revenue in FY2025 was flat, but revenue from cybersecurity grew 7% YoY, driven by more demand from its enterprise customers.

In my opinion, that growth in cybersecurity isn’t happening in a vacuum—the CAGR for the global cybersecurity market is 7.5% over the next 4 years. Cybercrimes are becoming more sophisticated and happening far more often, and it’s obvious that the main drivers are geopolitical tensions and rapid advances in automation, AI, and machine learning. In fact, “...nearly 60% of organizations state that geopolitical tensions have affected their cybersecurity strategy,” according to the World Economic Forum’s Global Cybersecurity Outlook for 2025.

So, then, the demand for solutions like NetScout’s Adaptive DDoS and Mobile Security is increasing. Everyone, from enterprises to service providers, is investing a lot in scalable, AI-augmented cybersecurity measures.

Now, if I look at the enterprise market, which NetScout services with its network performance and visibility solutions, the picture here is rosy, too. More businesses are investing in digital transformation and better network visibility. It’s not a short-term trend because more organizations want to improve their edge monitoring and 5G network operations, two key areas where NetScout is a market leader.

Moving on to specific product offerings, NetScout’s Sightline Detection, TMS Mitigation Solutions, and Smart Data from Deep Packet Inspection are incredibly valuable to its new and existing customers. And we can see this with its contracts, like the mid-seven-figure deal the company just signed with a leading cloud service provider. That deal expands an existing relationship and is supposed to improve the customer’s network and data security using NetScout’s Sightline detection and TMS mitigation solutions. I think it’s reasonable to say that deals like these show us that the company is seeing success with bigger, more integrated solutions in the cloud services and data center sectors.

The way I see it, that strong momentum in cybersecurity and the enterprise market represents the long-term value NetScout is building. The company’s solutions portfolio is pretty extensive and well-received, and it’d be fair to say it will capture a larger market share over the next few years. It makes sense that we remember that, even when accounting for slowdowns in its legacy segments like Service Assurance, where revenue dropped by around 4% in FY2025. Even with that, management is still confident that its service provider customers will “invest in 5G initiatives at a measured pace.”

Leadership Continuity is Crucial

During the company’s last earnings call on May 8, 2025, CEO Anil Singhal announced that COO Michael Szabados and CFO Jean Bua are retiring at the end of the month. At the same time, their deputies will move into those positions—Sanjay Munshi is set to step into the COO role, and Tony Piazza will take over as CFO.

Big news, yes. For one, most stakeholders didn’t exactly expect the news at this time. Still, it’s the logical move, and I don’t think that the continuity in leadership can be overemphasized, especially for a company like NetScout. I’m particularly confident that the company knows what it’s doing because the well-planned succession strategy means that it has avoided unnecessary uncertainty within the organization. I also can’t overstate how important it is for the culture when employees and the industry can see that the company invests in maintaining a strong internal leadership pipeline.

Also, the leadership transition coming now, when NetScout is making big moves in its cybersecurity and enterprise services business, demonstrates its stability. We can see that the company is well-placed to continue executing on its current strategy without any disruption because the new crop of leaders have been active in shaping its direction for years.

In my view, the smooth transition is vital to maintaining investor confidence. After all, NetScout is focused on building its cybersecurity portfolio and becoming more profitable, so having leaders who can execute its long-term goals is ideal. As an investor, knowing that there’s continuity within the company is encouraging. Why? It ensures that the company’s strategic priorities and operational efficiency are intact.

The Numbers Don’t Lie

The last thing I want to discuss is NetScout’s position in the market. Granted, the company still has a major chunk of its revenue coming from selling communications hardware, but over 80% of its revenue now comes from software sales. This is especially important when we consider the impact that tariffs can have on a business that generates 57% of its revenue in the US and the other 43% in international markets.

The positive thing, though, is that reliance on software sales means that NetScout has little to no exposure to tariff impacts and supply chain disruption. On the hardware side, it’s still safe because of USMCA exemptions for its hardware components. Of course, shifting towards software and recurring revenue hasn’t happened overnight. Still, it’s paying off now because the company can rest easy knowing that its revenue streams are more stable and predictable, regardless of the general macroeconomic environment.

Now, I want to talk about NetScout’s finances. Generally speaking, it’s in rude financial health. Free cash flow for FY2025 was $211 million; it also repaid the outstanding $75 million on its $600 million revolver. Even more importantly, this is a company that recorded revenues of $823 million and a GAAP net loss of around $368 million in FY2025, albeit that came with “total non-cash goodwill charges of $427.0 million.” By any metrics, maintaining that revenue figure compared to FY2024, which was relatively taxing, is impressive.

Having said all that, I’m fairly optimistic about its future prospects., Management’s guidance for FY2026 says that revenue will grow by ~5%, and GAAP net income should come in between $80 and $90 million, and there’s no reason to doubt that. So, on the whole, the company is heading towards profitability within the next two years. That’s not something to sleep on, given everything we know about the general state of the communications and cybersecurity industries.

Valuation

In my view, the picture hasn't changed much regarding NTCT's growth potential. Yes, the stock price has even dipped a bit since February, but my confidence comes from it being relatively undervalued in market terms. The company's non-GAAP forward P/E ratio is still around 10x, lower than that of Extreme Networks (EXTR) (13x).

Risks

Let me be clear here: there is only one major thing I want to watch out for when it comes to how NetScout can stay on course. That would be the overall slowdown in demand from the telecoms industry. However, management has already shared that they expect it and it shouldn't affect the bottom line. Demand from customers for other solutions, like 5G initiatives and digital transformation systems will still be steady, so I'm unbothered by any revenue drop from the telcos.

Tariff risks are there, on some level, especially with how randomly the current US administration keeps announcing and then backtracking on enforcement. On the whole, though, NTCT looks safe on that front, so the general mood looks to be one of cautious, justifiable optimism.

Your Takeaway

TL;DR: NetScout is moving strategically in a positive direction, and all the signs are there that it is an appealing long-term investment. Why? Well,

  • Its leadership transition is well-managed,

  • Its key drivers are in high-demand industries,

  • It is insulated from tariff impacts, and

  • Its operations are built on a solid business model

In my view, the play here is simple: the company is a good option to explore if you want to capitalize on long-term growth in cybersecurity and enterprise services. And right now, the stock appears undervalued, even more so when you see my breakdown of its potential in my previous article.

data.title: Bayer Stock Investment Could Pay Off, But It Is Not For The Faint Of Heart
data.description:
A lot has happened since my last article about Bayer A.G. (OTCPK:BAYZF, OTCPK:BAYRY). The company’s stock price is trading near multi-year lows. And the company has recently published its earnings results, which have disappointed many investors. In my view, the company’s turnaround could make the stock price surge. But the question is when it will be. The stock is very cheap, but it is not appropriate for conservative investors.

Recap of my previous article about Bayer

In my previous article, I wrote that Bayer's stock was undervalued and offered potential for long-term recovery. Bayer’s were quite strong for a while. But earnings did not show sustainable growth. The company’s key growth stimuli included resolving glyphosate and new drugs. The risks were litigation expenses and debt. I wrote that the recent set of results was only a temporary decline.

However, the decline is still ongoing, and it is not clear when it will be over. At the same time, on the bright side, we see that cash flow from operations is rising. But let me go into more details about the company’s earnings results.

Bayer’s earnings results

Bayer’s Q1 2025 profitability results came in below expectations. However, the corporation’s cash flow figures were substantially better compared to the same period a year ago.

Here are some of the key numbers:

The group sales totaled €13.74 billion, a 0.2% decline year-on-year. This was not much, but there was no growth either. The crop science division performed poorly, while the pharmaceuticals posted higher sales and earnings. The group EBITDA before special items declined by 7.4% to €4,085 million. However, the group EBITDA margin before special items totaled 29.7%.

The core earnings per share totaled EUR 2.49 (-11.7%), and the net income also declined. The free cash flow totaled minus EUR 1.5 billion, a substantial improvement compared to the same period a year ago. In Q1 2024, it totaled minus €2,626 million, mainly due to the improvement in operating cash flow.

As you can see above, I have circled the key income statement data. The profitability figures have substantially declined; the sales have remained almost unchanged, while the cash flow deficit has fortunately decreased.

Here are the key corporate events announced by the company.

In March this year, Bayer announced that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration had granted a Priority Review designation for Kerendia™, a medicine used for the treatment of adult patients suffering from heart failure with a left ventricular ejection fraction of more than 40%.

In April this year, the company also submitted an application for a third indication of Eylea™ 8 mg with the European Medicines Agency. It is likely that the medicine used to treat patients with macular edema following retinal vein occlusion will be approved, given the positive results from the global Phase III QUASAR study.

Among the efficiency measures in the crop science division was the company’s decision to reorganize the division’s activities in the areas of crop protection, production, and research and development in Germany. As part of these changes, the division will stop its operations in Frankfurt am Main after the end of 2028. Bayer aims to sell parts of this division. The rest of them will be relocated. Production at the Dormagen site will be changed to ensure its competitiveness for the future.

So, as concerns the earnings performance of the Bayer Group, sales in Germany amounted to €791 million, a rise compared to the Q1 2024 result of €729 million.

Sales at the crop science division, meanwhile, decreased year-on-year due to regulatory impacts in the U.S. and the EU, as well as volume phasing into the second quarter. Sales at the pharmaceuticals division increased, thanks to Nubeqa™, a medicine used to treat prostate cancer, and Kerendia™. The company also recorded continued sales revenue growth for Eylea™ and the radiology business, and strong performance in the contraceptives division. These positive effects were partially offset by falling sales of Xarelto™, a medicine used to reduce heart problem risks, namely, heart attack and stroke, in adults with coronary artery disease.

The company’s consumer health division also registered a rise in sales.

In the context of Bayer’s quarterly earnings history, the recent results were not a catastrophe.

Bayer’s results history (in millions of USD except per share items)

As can be seen from the graph below, the revenues since 2022 have not changed much. The operating income has decreased since 2023 but has remained at the level recorded in 2022.

Bayer’s results history (in millions of USD, except per share items)

The company’s recent news and financial fundamentals

Bayer’s stock price decreased during Wednesday's trading session as the U.S. Health and Human Services Secretary Kennedy's planned "Make America Healthy Again" report is expected to single out glyphosate, Bayer’s commonly used Roundup weedkiller. The report is expected to label pesticides as a potential cause of U.S. health problems.

The crop science division, as I have mentioned above, is about to be reformed by Bayer’s management. Yet, glyphosate-based herbicides are accountable for EUR 2,647 million of the crop science division’s sales. So, a ban on glyphosate could be a real problem.

Let me go into more details about Bayer’s financial fundamentals. In the years 2023 and 2024, the company’s annual net profits totaled ($3,246.9) million and ($2,643.7) million, respectively. The net profit TTM totals ($3,519.4) million.

As concerns the company’s balance sheet, it is not in the best shape either.

The company’s net debt only totaled $38.27 billion, much higher than the company’s competitors, whose stock tickers are given in the table below. The debt-to-equity ratio, though not too high—it should not be above 200%—is much higher than most of the companies’ peers.

The current and the covered ratios also do not suggest ample amounts of liquidity, even though the company is easily able to pay back interest on its debt.

However, Bayer’s stock seems to be undervalued compared to its peers.

Apart from the profitability ratios that are impossible to generate thanks to Bayer’s lack of net profitability, the rest of the valuation figures, including the P/B, the EV/Sales, and the EV/EBITDA, suggest substantial undervaluation compared to the sector’s median.

Also, if we have a look at Bayer’s stock price history, we will see that the company’s stock is lingering near all-time lows.

So, Bayer’s stock does not look expensive.

But for Bayer’s shares not to fall further, the company needs a turnaround as soon as possible.

Conclusion

To summarize, Bayer has several divisions, namely the pharmaceuticals and the consumer health divisions, that record rising sales. The company also plans to reform its crop sciences division to decrease the losses. On the positive side, we can also say that Bayer’s sales are not falling. Moreover, during the last quarter, the cash flow deficit has decreased. At the same time, in my view, Bayer stock is not appropriate for conservative investors. The turnaround is not certain, while the already cheap Bayer A.G. stock can still keep depreciating if the company does not start reporting improving profitability figures.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
